author: Bhavith S
tone: concerned, assertive, protective of retail interests
executive_summary: Bhavith S, a retail investor, strongly opposes the proposed rescission of Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) under Regulation NMS. The author argues that removing the Trade-Through Rule will eliminate a critical hard mechanical safeguard for retail investors, enabling zero-commission brokerages to prioritize Payment for Order Flow (PFOF) revenue over best execution. The author draws on personal experience with execution discrepancies and analogizes the risk to unregulated cryptocurrency markets.

positions.best_execution: Opposes reliance on subjective best execution standard as a replacement for Rule 611; views it as insufficient protection compared to a hard mathematical rule
positions.transparency: Implicitly supports price transparency through retention of NBBO-based routing requirements

economic_arguments: Zero-commission brokerages have inherent conflicts of interest because they generate revenue through PFOF rather than commissions, misaligning their incentives with retail investor outcomes
Without Rule 611, brokerages will be financially incentivized to route orders to venues that pay the highest PFOF rather than venues offering the best price
A hard mathematical rule provides stronger and more reliable investor protection than a subjective qualitative standard such as best execution
liquidity_arguments: Author does not make explicit liquidity arguments but implicitly suggests that off-exchange routing to wholesalers may not provide retail investors with access to the best publicly displayed prices
competition_arguments: Removal of Rule 611 would favor corporate middlemen and off-exchange wholesalers over retail investors
PFOF-driven routing decisions would distort competitive order routing away from price-optimal venues toward revenue-optimal venues for brokerages
retail_investor_arguments: Retail investors already experience execution price discrepancies relative to displayed prices under the current regime
Rescinding Rule 611 risks replicating the unregulated, slippage-prone conditions observed in cryptocurrency markets within U.S. equities
The NBBO-based Trade-Through Rule is the primary mechanical guarantee that retail trades receive the best publicly displayed price
Subjective best execution standards are inadequate substitutes for a hard, enforceable price protection rule
Retail investors are particularly vulnerable to broker conflicts of interest at zero-commission platforms such as Robinhood and Webull
recommended_actions: Retain Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) in its current form
Do not rescind the Trade-Through Rule under Regulation NMS
Maintain the hard mechanical NBBO price protection standard for retail order routing
key_quotes: I am deeply concerned that repealing Rule 611 will bring that same lack of strict price protection to the U.S. stock market, exacerbating the problem for everyday traders.

If the strict mandate to route orders to the venue displaying the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) is removed, these brokerages will be highly incentivized to route retail orders to favored off-exchange wholesalers and alternative venues that pay them the most in Payment for Order Flow (PFOF).
Without the rigid mechanical protection of the Trade-Through Rule, brokerages will prioritize their own kickbacks and revenue over securing the absolute best execution price for their customers.
The subjective 'best execution' standard is not a sufficient replacement for a hard, mathematical rule that guarantees retail investors get the best publicly displayed price.
Please do not remove the very safeguard that ensures retail trades are not traded-through for the benefit of corporate middlemen.
